coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from ascorbic acid and their application in sensing and biological imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Xiao-Hua
An, Xueqin
coredata.dc:description: A simple, highly efficient, and environmentally friendly one-step method has been developed to synthesize fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with the assistance of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Here H2O2 acts as a green accelerator to promote the preparation of CDs, while temperature plays a critical role in controlling the entire synthesis process. The prepared CDs demonstrate monodispersion, with an average diameter of ∼6.8 nm, superior stability, and excitation-dependent photoluminescent (PL) behavior. The PL mechanism of the CDs was investigated using time-resolved PL decay, revealing that the PL originates from both the defect state and the intrinsic state of the CDs. Furthermore, the CDs show significant promise as pH sensors and versatile nanothermometry devices, owing to their remarkable sensitivity to both pH and temperature, as demonstrated by the pronounced dependence of their steady-state fluorescence emission spectra. It’s worth noting that temperature and pH sensors present significant reversibility, sensitivity, and linearity. More importantly, the CDs display low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and the ability to enter human breast cancer Bcap-37 cells, making them highly suitable candidates for cellular imaging and labeling.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Green synthesis method
Photoluminescent mechanism
Sensors
Biological imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adjusting the pH Value of CDs’ aqueous solution
Fluorescence responses of CDs to pH
Fluorescence responses of CDs to temperature

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs, inset: size distributions of CDs. (b) High-resolution TEM image of CDs.
XRD patterns of (a) CDs and (b) L-ascorbic acid. FT-IR spectra of (c) CDs and (d) L-ascorbic acid.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectrum of CDs. (b) Normalized PL spectra at excitation wavelengths ranging from 330 to 490 nm with a 20 nm increment.

(a) The PL spectrum of CDs (black solid line), band-I (blue dashed line) and band-II (green dashed line) are PL spectrum fitted by two-Gaussian functions, suggesting that the spectrum is composed of two different emission mechanisms, inset: schematic representation of dual emission mechanisms in CDs. (b) PL decay curves of CDs in the aqueous solution at room temperature under different emission wavelengths.

(a) PL spectra of CDs measured at pH values ranging from 3–9 with a 1 increment. (b) A linear relationship between PL intensity and pH values, inset: PL intensity upon the cyclic switching of CDs under alternating conditions of pH = 3 and pH = 9.

(a) PL spectra of CDs measured at temperatures ranging from 20 °C to 80 °C with a 5 °C increment. (b) A linear relationship between PL intensity and temperatures, inset: PL intensity of CDs measured at 20 °C and 80 °C after undergoing repeated heating-cooling cycles.
(a) The effect of CDs on Bcap-37 cells viability (MTT assay). Confocal fluorescence microphotographs of CDs labelled Bcap-37 cells under bright field (b) and at excitation wavelength of 405 nm (c).
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of CD in aqueous solution at 80°C.
The expression of the fitted functions and average fluorescence lifetime.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: A and τ correspond to the normalized amplitude and decay time constant, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fitting parameters of the corresponding PL decay curves.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: λ ex is the excitation wavelength, λ em is the emission wavelength, χ 2 is the value of the goodness-of-fit parameter.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from ascorbic acid and their application in sensing and biological imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Information Science and Technology, Fudan University, 220 Handan Road, Shanghai 200433, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Information Science and Technology, Fudan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Information Science and Technology, Fudan University, 220 Handan Road, Shanghai 200433, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, 130 Meilong Road, Shanghai 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, 130 Meilong Road, Shanghai 200237, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, highly efficient, and environmentally friendly one-step method has been developed to synthesize fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with the assistance of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Here H2O2 acts as a green accelerator to promote the preparation of CDs, while temperature plays a critical role in controlling the entire synthesis process. The prepared CDs demonstrate monodispersion, with an average diameter of ∼6.8 nm, superior stability, and excitation-dependent photoluminescent (PL) behavior. The PL mechanism of the CDs was investigated using time-resolved PL decay, revealing that the PL originates from both the defect state and the intrinsic state of the CDs. Furthermore, the CDs show significant promise as pH sensors and versatile nanothermometry devices, owing to their remarkable sensitivity to both pH and temperature, as demonstrated by the pronounced dependence of their steady-state fluorescence emission spectra. It’s worth noting that temperature and pH sensors present significant reversibility, sensitivity, and linearity. More importantly, the CDs display low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and the ability to enter human breast cancer Bcap-37 cells, making them highly suitable candidates for cellular imaging and labeling.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: To date, fluorescence is still one of the most powerful and commonly employed tools for biological research [1–3] . Thanks to versatile fluorescent materials, we are enabled to observe and understand life at the molecular and cellular levels, from illuminating cellular processes to tracking the dynamics of biomolecules [2]. The fluorescent materials employed include organic dyes and conventional semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) [4,5] . Nevertheless, organic dyes often suffer from poor photostability, while SQDs have the main pitfall of their potential cytotoxicity, restricting their use in biological applications [3–6] . To address these limitations, a new class of nanomaterials known as fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) is emerging, offering a promising alternative [4–6] . These fluorescent CDs show unique properties, including small sizes, high water solubility, excellent photostability, low toxicity, great biocompatibility, and ease of surface functionalization [5,7–10]. Due to these advantages, CDs have been widely used in various fields, ranging from electronic devices to biosensors and bioimaging agents [5,11–14]. In particular, CDs have emerged as highly promising candidates for pH and temperature sensing applications due to their remarkable sensitivity, biocompatibility, chemical stability, and cost-effectiveness [6,15–17]. They enable accurate and rapid detection of subtle changes in microenvironmental pH and temperature, thus facilitating real-time monitoring applications [6,17]. For instance, Ye et al. have demonstrated the successful application of fluorescent CDs for sensing and visualizing pH fluctuations in cells, tissues, and zebrafish [15]. Macairan et al. showed that fluorescent CDs can be used for thermal sensing inside HeLa cancer cells [16].

Over the past two decades, numerous approaches have been developed for preparing fluorescent CDs, including the hydrothermal method [18–21], microwave synthesis [22–24], ultrasonic treatment [25,26], arc discharge [27], laser irradiation [28], electrochemical synthesis [29–31], and chemical oxidation [32]. However, many of these methods suffer from drawbacks such as complex synthetic processes, high temperature and high-pressure requirements, time-consuming procedures, expensive apparatus, and high costs, all of which have limited their practical applications. On the other hand, several methods utilizing various alkalis (NaOH [33–35], NaHCO3 [34], and KOH [33]), acids (H2SO4 [23,36], HNO3 [23,32,37], and HCl [23]), oxides (P2O5 [38,39]) and metal ion (Cu2+ [40]) assisted methods have been reported for the synthesis of CDs. However, it’s important to note that these assisted reagents have significant drawbacks. They are highly corrosive, pose a danger to health, are uneconomical, and environmentally unfriendly. Furthermore, CDs produced using these methods often require a laborious post-treatment process, involving centrifugation, dialysis, or other separation techniques for purification. For example, Li et al. reported the preparation of CDs by mixing saccharide and base; however, this method required more than a day of dialysis for purification, and the quantum yield (QY) of CDs was only 1.2% ∼ 2.2% [34]. Babar et al. developed the P2O5-assisted synthesis of CDs from sugar, which needed centrifugation and multiple washing processes to remove side products [38]. Jia et al. synthesized CDs from L-ascorbic acid using Cu2+ as a catalyst, but this approach also required centrifugation to remove byproducts such as Cu or Cu2O, and the QY of CDs was also relatively low at 3.2% [40]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a green method for synthesizing CDs that is not only straightforward and simple but also highly efficient, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly, all while ensuring excellent optical properties.

In this work, we present a green, simple, and novel method for synthesizing highly efficient CDs using L-ascorbic acid. This method involves a one-step treatment at low temperatures, assisted by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The synthesis mechanism of the CDs has been investigated. In addition, the photoluminescent (PL) origin of the CDs has also been explored using both steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence techniques. The attractive CDs can serve as nanoprobes for sensing temperature and pH in aqueous solutions, showcasing their versatility. Furthermore, the CDs have demonstrated low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, rendering them well-suited for a variety of biomedical applications.

L-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, 99%) was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China) and used directly without any purification. H2O2 (30%), HCl (37%), NaOH (99.7%), NaCl (99.7%) MgCl2·6H2O (99%), AlCl3 (98%), ZnCl2 (98%), KCl (99%), PbCl2 (98%), CoCl2 (97%), CrCl3 (99%), CaCl2 (97%), BaCl2 (99%), MnCl2 (98%) and CdCl2 (98%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All aqueous solutions were prepared using double-distilled water.

In a typical experiment, 0.25 g of L-ascorbic acid was dissolved in 50 mL of double-distilled water, resulting in a clear solution. Subsequently, 0.25 mL of 30% H2O2 was added to the solution. The mixture was stirred for 5 min at room temperature and then incubated at 80 °C for 3 h in a water bath without disturbance. Finally, highly fluorescent CDs were prepared.
Fluorescent CDs were also obtained without the addition of H2O2 by extending the reaction time to 100 h at 80 °C.

A JEOL JEM 2100 microscope was used to obtain the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the CDs. To investigate the crystal structure of the CDs, a Rigaku X-ray diffraction (XRD) instrument was utilized. A Perkin Elmer BX Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer was performed to obtain the FT-IR spectrum of the CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi) was employed to determine the elemental composition of CDs. The UV–visible spectra of the CDs were recorded using a Hitachi UV2450 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra of the CDs were measured using an Edinburgh F900 fluorescence spectrometer. Cell imaging, utilizing the CDs as fluorescence labels, was investigated under a Leica DM6000 CS laser scanning confocal microscope.

The QY of the CDs can be calculated using the following equation [3]:
(1)
Φ
X
=
Φ
S
×
M
X
M
S
×
η
X
2
η
S
2

In this equation, the subscript S represents the standard reference (quinine sulfate with a QY of 54% in a 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 solution) and the subscript X denotes the tested sample (the prepared CDs). Φ represents the QY, M is the slope from the plot of the integrated PL intensity against the absorbance of the sample or the standard at various concentrations, and η is the refractive index of the solvent.

The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of the CDs was determined through time-resolved PL measurements. The decay traces were fitted using monoexponential or biexponential functions represented by I(t). The specific functions used for fitting, as well as the average fluorescence lifetime (t ag), as listed in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adjusting the pH Value of CDs’ aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: HCl (2 mol/L) or NaOH (2 mol/L) were employed to modulate the pH of the aqueous solution containing the CDs.

In each well, 100 μL of a suspension of human breast cancer Bcap-37 cells was added, and they were incubated in a 5% CO2 humidified incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. These cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium, supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin streptomycin. Subsequently, the culture medium was replaced with fresh medium containing CDs at various concentrations ranging from 0.05 mg/mL to 0.8 mg/mL), and the cells were further incubated for 24 h. Following the incubation period, the culture medium was removed, and the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). Then, 20 μL of a 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated for an another 4 h, leading to the formation of a violet-colored formazan product. After the MTT incubation, the culture medium was removed, and 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were added to each well. The resulting mixture was shaken for 10 min, and the absorbance at 490 nm was measured using a Bio-Rad model-680 microplate reader. Cell viability was determined by calculating the absorbance percentage relative to the control sample (cells without exposure to CDs) using the following equation:
(2)
Cell viability =
A
s
A
c
×
100 %
Where the A s represents the absorbance of cells incubated with CDs, and A c denotes the absorbance of cells incubated without CDs, both measured at 490 nm. All the experiments were carried out in triplicate, and the mean values were considered for analysis.

2 mL of human breast cancer Bcap-37 cells were initially seeded in a cover glass-bottom dish and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. These cells were maintained in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium, supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin streptomycin. Subsequently, the culture medium was replaced with 2 mL of fresh medium containing CDs at a concentration of 0.8 mg/mL, and the cells were incubated at 37 °C for an additional 24 h. Following this incubation period, the culture medium was carefully removed, and the cells were subjected to three washes with PBS. Cell imaging was then conducted using a laser scanning confocal microscopy, employing laser excitation wavelengths of 405 nm, and the emitted fluorescence in the blue region was collected for analysis.

In this method, L-ascorbic acid was initially dissolved in water, followed by the addition of H2O2 to the solution. The resulting mixture was stirred for 5 min at room temperature and was subsequently maintained at 80 °C for 3 h in a water bath without any disruption. After the reaction, the initially colorless mixture transformed into a light yellow solution, indicating the successful formation of CDs.

To elucidate the formation process, we have proposed a plausible mechanism for the generation of CDs from L-ascorbic acid based on the experimental results. The mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 1. L-ascorbic acid is known to be very unstable in aqueous solutions, particularly when in the presence of heat, light, and oxidants [40–44]. The degradation scheme of L-ascorbic acid in aqueous solutions was determined by liquid chromatography coupled with triple quadrupole mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [42], as depicted in Figure S1. Specifically, L-ascorbic acid first oxidizes reversibly to form monodehydro-L-ascorbic acid, which further undergoes reversibly oxidized to produce dehydro-L-ascorbic acid. Then, dehydro-L-ascorbic acid degrades irreversibly to 2,3-diketogulonic acid. Finally, the 2,3-diketogulonic acid degrades irreversibly to the formation of 2,3,4-trihydroxybutanoic acid and 1,2-ethanedioic acid. The degradation pathway of L-ascorbic acid was also corroborated by Lloyd et al. using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [41]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the 2,3,4-trihydroxybutanoic acid and 1,2-ethanedioic acid are the final degradation products of L-ascorbic acid in aqueous solutions.

It is noteworthy that the degradation of L-ascorbic acid in aqueous solutions is an oxidative degradation process. Consequently, the introduction of certain oxidizing agents can markedly expedite this degradation. Given that H2O2 is a potent oxidizing agent, its presence undoubtedly accelerates the oxidative degradation rate of L-ascorbic acid in aqueous solutions. On the other hand, temperature emerges as a crucial factor influencing the degradation of L-ascorbic acid in aqueous solution, with degradation occurring even at room temperature [42]. Higher temperatures further hasten the degradation process. In our method, we not only introduce strong oxidants (i.e., H2O2) into the L-ascorbic acid solution but also elevate the reaction temperature. The combination of these strategies, involving both strong oxidants and higher temperature, greatly enhances the degradation of L-ascorbic acid in aqueous solution.

Based on the above analysis, we proposed a possible mechanism of the formation for CDs derived from L-ascorbic acid in aqueous solutions. Initially, L-ascorbic acid undergoes very rapid oxidization, generating some intermediate products (e.g., dehydro-L-ascorbic acid and 2,3-diketogulonic acid) in the presence of H2O2 at 80 °C. Immediately afterwards, the final products, 2,3,4-trihydroxybutanoic acid, and 1,2-ethanedioic acid, are formed [41,42]. The concentration of these final products sharply increases, reaching a supersaturation point due to the rapid degradation. However, both 2,3,4-trihydroxybutanoic acid and 1,2-ethanedioic acid are unstable under the given conditions of supersaturation and higher temperature (80 °C) and readily undergo polymerization through intermolecular and intramolecular dehydration processes. This leads to the formation of polymer-like CDs. Subsequently, these polymer-like CDs undergo further dehydration at 80 °C. Eventually, highly fluorescent CDs are formed. The synthesis mechanism of CDs in our study exhibits similarities to those reported previously [3,34,35]. In addition, it has been reported that one-pot fabrication of fluorescent CDs by directly heating L-ascorbic acid aqueous solution at 90 °C in the presence of Cu2+ [40]. They claim that these CDs are synthesized through ‘‘polymerization’’ and ‘‘carbonization’’ steps. The key distinction between our method and theirs lies in the choice of oxidizing agents: they utilize Cu2+, while we employ H2O2. Taken together, the formation mechanism of CDs involves the initial degradation of L-ascorbic acid to produce small molecules, followed by subsequent polymerization and dehydration processes, as shown in Scheme 1.

Importantly, this simple, straightforward, environmentally friendly, and highly efficient synthesis method does not require any complex post-treatment processes, as H2O2 is converted to H2O, and L-ascorbic acid transforms into CDs during the reaction. Compared to the Cu2+-assisted method [40], our H2O2-assisted approach offers two advantages: it does not require complex post-processing processes such as centrifugation, and it does not introduce heavy metal copper elements, thereby truly realizing the green synthesis of fluorescent CDs. The morphology and size of the prepared CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1, the CDs presented spherical particle with monodispersity, and the average diameter is approximately 6.8 nm (inset of Fig. 1 a). Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1 b) shows that the CDs synthesized using this method exhibit an amorphous carbon structure. The absence of a well-defined lattice structure suggests that the CDs consist of carbon atoms arranged in a disordered manner, contributing to their unique optical and electronic properties.

Interestingly, it was observed that when the L-ascorbic acid aqueous solution was directly treated at 80 °C for 100 h, fluorescent CDs were also obtained (Figure S2). In an aqueous solution, L-ascorbic acid undergoes heat-induced degradation, ultimately forming small molecules such as 2,3,4-trihydroxybutanoic acid and 1,2-ethanedioic acid [41,42]. However, the degradation process is relatively slow. This explains why the formation of CDs without the addition of H2O2 required a much longer reaction time of about 100 h. These results indicate that H2O2 significantly accelerates the oxidation of L-ascorbic acid during the formation process of CDs. Similar to previous reports, the addition of Cu2+ was found to significantly expedite the process by accelerating the oxidation of L-ascorbic acid to generate CDs [40]. Moreover, it is worth noting that even without the presence of H2O2, the strategy to obtain CDs from L-ascorbic acid still resulted in the formation of spherical particles with an average diameter of approximately 6.6 nm (Figure S3).

In addition, the reaction temperature also plays a crucial role in the synthesis of CDs. As depicted in Figure S4a, the PL intensity of the CDs increased with higher reaction temperatures. This can be attributed to the fact that higher temperatures accelerate the processes of oxidization, polymerization, dehydration, leading to the formation of a larger quantity of CDs. The color of the aqueous solution also deepened with increasing reaction temperature, indicating an increase in the amount of CDs [40]. Furthermore, the UV-visible absorption spectra of the CDs (Figure S4b) revealed that within the temperature range of 50 °C ∼ 70 °C, the aqueous solution did not exhibit the characteristic absorption peak of CDs but rather a strong broad absorption band in the range of 225 nm ∼ 263 nm. However, at 80 °C or 90 °C, the UV-visible spectra exhibited a typical absorption peak of CDs, indicating the complete conversion of L-ascorbic acid into fluorescent CDs.

Overall, the addition of H2O2 significantly reduced the process time by accelerating the oxidization of L-ascorbic acid, resulting in the formation of highly reactive precursors that facilitated the synthesis of CDs. The temperature (80 °C) played a crucial role in controlling the entire process of CDs synthesis.

We performed XRD measurements to investigate the crystalline properties of the CDs, and the results are presented in Fig. 2 a. Notably, a broad diffraction peak was observed, centered at 2θ ≈ 23.8°. It confirms that the CDs are composed of an amorphous carbon structure, consistent with the results of high-resolution TEM in Fig. 1 b. Using Bragg’s equation, the interlayer spacing of the CDs was calculated to be about 0.37 nm. This larger interlayer spacing compared to bulk graphite (0.34 nm) was attributed to the abundance of functional groups present on the surfaces of the CDs [36,45]. It is worth noting that L-ascorbic acid is a highly crystalline molecule and shows several very strong diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern (Fig. 2 b). In contrast, the XRD pattern of the as-prepared CDs did not show any diffraction peaks associated with L-ascorbic acid. This indicates that L-ascorbic acid has been completely converted into CDs.

XPS analysis was conducted to determine the elemental composition of CDs. In Figure S5a, the full XPS spectrum of CDs exhibits two prominent peaks corresponding to C 1 s and O 1 s, indicating the presence of carbon and oxygen elements without any other impurities. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum in Figure S5b can be deconvoluted into three different signals at ∼284.8 eV, ∼288.3 eV, and ∼286.2 eV. The dominant CC/C–C peak at ∼284.8 eV corresponding to the sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms in graphitic structure [3,40,46–49]. The remaining two peaks at ∼286.2 eV and ∼288.3 eV are assigned to carbon atom in the C–O and CO functional groups, respectively [3,40,46,49].

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to analyze the composition of the carbon core and functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared CDs. Figs. 2 c and 2 d displays the IR spectra of L-ascorbic acid and CDs, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs possesses an absorption peak at 1646 cm−1, suggesting the formation of CC unsaturated bonds in the carbon cores [3,16,40]. Furthermore, numerous distinctive hydrophilic functional groups are evident on the surface of the CDs, as illustrated by the vibration bands of O−H (∼3350 cm−1), CO (∼1745 cm−1), and C–O (∼1200 cm−1) groups. These oxygen-containing functional groups are believed to originate from L-ascorbic acid as their carbon source and they are likely responsible for the efficient PL by trapping excitons under excitation and the radiative recombination of those surface-trapped excitons. Importantly, these hydrophilic groups not only greatly enhanced the solubility of the CDs in aqueous systems and improved their stability, but also significantly expanded their potential applications in the fields of biological and biomedical sciences.

The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the CDs in the aqueous solution is shown in Fig. 3 a, exhibiting two prominent absorption peaks in the UV region. The peak centered at 242 nm is ascribed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic CC band originating from the carbon core of CDs [3,13,50], whereas the shoulder at 285 nm is assigned to the n-π* transition of the CO groups present on the CDs’ surface [3,13,40,50–52].

The PL spectra of the CDs were investigated under various excitation wavelengths, as depicted in Fig. 3 b. An excitation-dependent emission behaviour was observed. Specifically, as the excitation wavelengths ranged from 330 nm to 490 nm, the corresponding emission wavelengths shifted from 410 nm to 560 nm. This behaviour can be attributed to the presence of multiple active and independently excitable emission sites within the structure of CDs [50,53,54]. The PL QY of the CDs in the aqueous solution was measured to be about 11.86% (Figure S6 and Table S1), which was nearly 4 times higher than that of CDs obtained using a similar method (with Cu2+ as a catalyst) and the same carbon source [40]. In additionally, it’s worth noting that the PL intensity of the CDs remained nearly constant even in the presence of varying NaCl concentrations, ranging from 0 to 2 mol/L (Figure S7). The results indicate that the prepared CDs exhibit remarkable stability even in high-salt conditions. At the same time, we also examined whether certain metal ions in the environment would interfere with the fluorescence of CDs. The results indicate that metal ions, such as Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Pb2+, Co2+, and Cu2+, do not significantly affect the PL intensity of CDs (Figure S8). To investigate the photobleaching resistance of CDs, continuous irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp for one hour was conducted. It was found that the PL intensity of the CDs still remained at 98% (Figure S9), indicating excellent photostability of the CDs. Moreover, the CDs exhibited long-term fluorescence stability, maintaining a high level of fluorescence for over a year at room temperature. These findings highlight the exceptional stability of the CDs and their promising potential for future applications.

The PL spectrum of the CDs, depicted by the solid black line in Fig. 4 a, displays an asymmetric curve with a tailing effect in the long-wavelength region. This characteristic strongly suggests the presence of multiple luminescent species within the CDs [55–57]. In the analysis of the PL spectrum, we employed a multi-Gaussian function for fitting. Remarkably, the PL spectrum could be well fitted using a two-Gaussian function. This fitting unveiled that the high-energy band exhibited a peak centered at 410 nm (referred to as band-I, illustrated by the blue dashed line in Fig. 4 a), whereas the low-energy band displayed a peak centered at 460 nm (referred to as band-II, represented by the green dashed line in Fig. 4 a). These findings indicate that the as-prepared CDs possess dual-luminescent species.

The radiative lifetime of emission is a crucial characteristic of light-emitting nanoparticles, as it can provide valuable insights into the electron-hole recombination mechanisms. Different radiative lifetimes are associated with distinct recombination processes [58,59]. Time-resolved, pulsed laser excitation techniques are particularly well-suited for probing the emission lifetime of nanoparticles.

To further explore the fluorescence origin of the CDs, the fluorescence lifetime was determined by time-resolved PL measurements. The decay trace was fitted using either monoexponential or biexponential functions I(t), as detailed in Table 1. The results of the PL decay fitting are presented in Fig. 4 b, and the corresponding fitting parameters are summarized in Table 2. The PL decay curves of the CDs exhibited a biexponential behaviour, with the exception of the case where λ ex/λ em = 330/410 nm, which showed a single exponential decay. The biexponential decay components can be attributed to recombination processes from the intrinsic states and the defect states of the CDs [60,61]. Typically, emissions originating from intrinsic states have short recombination lifetimes compared to those from surface defect states [30,55,58,59]. As shown in Table 2, the short lifetime (τ 1) of ∼1.70 ns might be attributed to the intrinsic state of the CDs, while the long lifetime (τ 2) ranging from 4.25 ns to 5.39 ns is likely associated with the surface defect state. Furthermore, the PL decay of the CDs was found to be dependent on the emission wavelength. As the emission wavelength shifted from 410 nm to 550 nm, it was observed that the normalized amplitude A 1 decreased from 100% to 71.85%, while A 2 increased from 0% to 28.15%, and the τ avg increased from 1.66 ns to 3.74 ns. These observations suggest that the PL decay behaviours of the CDs is intricately linked to the specific emission wavelength, signifying variations in the recombination dynamics of different luminescent species present in the CDs. In the case where λ ex/λ em = 330/410 nm, the PL decay of the CDs was fitted using a monoexponential function (Fig. 4 b), revealing a short lifetime of approximately 1.66 ns. This short lifetime suggests that the emission peak centered at 410 nm arises from the intrinsic states of the CDs, specifically the carbogenic core consisting of sp2 carbon nano domains embedded in a sp3 carbon matrix [57,59]. Consequently, the short lifetime (τ 1) can be attributed to the band-I emission peak centered at 410 nm (Fig. 4 a), while the long lifetime (τ 2) can be associated with the band-II emission peak centered at 460 nm (Fig. 4 a). Taken together, we can conclude that the CDs possess two distinct emission states: the short-wavelength emission originates from the intrinsic states (carbogenic core) of the CDs, while the long-wavelength emission is derived from the surface defect states associated with oxygenous functional groups on the CDs’ surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of CDs to pH
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH on the PL intensity of CDs remains a crucial factor to consider in relation to their fluorescence properties, as it can significantly affect their fluorescent stability and potential applications [17]. Currently, numerous research groups have successfully prepared CDs with pH-responsive fluorescence and have applied them to various biological applications, including sensing, drug delivery, and cell imaging [17,47].

In this work, the PL intensity of CDs prepared from L-ascorbic acid was found to be highly sensitive to changes in pH, as illustrated in Fig. 5 a. Specifically, as the pH values increased from 3 to 9, the PL intensity of the CDs gradually decreased from 100% to 39%. Our results align with reports indicating that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases with increasing pH [40,62]. This change in PL intensity was accompanied by a colour transformation in the CDs’ aqueous solution, shifting from light yellow to golden yellow. This suggests that the change in pH may induce electronic transition changes of π-π* and n-π* in graphite nanodomains by refilling or depleting their valence bands [40,63]. Furthermore, a notable linear correlation (R 2 = 0.995) was observed between the PL intensity of the CDs and the pH values within the range of 3–9 (Fig. 5 b). These findings suggest that the CDs have promising potential as outstanding pH-sensitive probes for biomedical applications, including pH sensing, drug delivery, and cell imaging. More importantly, we observed that the pH-induced fluorescent quenching effect was reversible. To assess the reversibility of the switching operation in response to pH variations, the CDs were subjected to pH cycling between pH = 3 and pH = 9 using HCl (2 mol/L) and NaOH (2 mol/L) as modulators. As shown in the inset of Fig. 5 b, the fluorescence switching operation could be repeated for six consecutive cycles without any signs of fatigue, demonstrating the excellent reversibility of the two-way switching processes. Based on these findings, we infer that the reversible protonation and deprotonation of oxygenated functional groups on the surface of CDs contribute to the pH dependency of CDs fluorescence [17,36,63]. The alterations in pH levels induce protonation and deprotonation events in these oxygenated functional groups, potentially leading to electrostatic doping/charging of the CDs and subsequently altering the Fermi level similar to graphene oxide nanosheets [63] and single-walled carbon nanotubes [64]. Nevertheless, further studies are needed to elucidate the detailed mechanisms in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of CDs to temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Temperature plays a crucial role in various biological processes [6,16]. It is imperative to obtain precise temperature measurements in living organisms with both high accuracy and specificity. This is essential for advancing fundamental cell studies and developing innovative diagnostic tools in biomedicine field. Fluorescent temperature-sensitive probes hold great promise for applications in the medical thermometry. Temperature information can be extracted based on various parameters such as fluorescence intensity, Stokes shift, or decay lifetime, all of which can be correlated with temperature [6,16]. Fluorescent CDs, with their unique characteristics including low toxicity, excellent photostability, and high biocompatibility, exhibit sensitive thermal properties [6]. This makes them excellent candidates in the biomedical field for nanoscale thermometric applications. Recent research has shown that CDs can serve as an effective thermometer in living cells [6,16,46].

Fig. 6 a clearly illustrates the significant temperature dependence of the emission spectra of the as-prepared CDs. The PL intensity decreased by 72% when the temperature was increased from 20 °C to 80 °C. This temperature-dependent behaviour was further demonstrated in Fig. 6 b, where the PL intensity of the CDs exhibited a near-linear correlation (R 2 = 0.995) with temperature across the temperature range from 20 °C to 80 °C. This linear correlation may facilitate the potential for CDs to regard as a temperature sensor. It’s worth noting that this temperature range exceeds the physiological temperature range, indicating the potential of the as-prepared CDs for in vivo temperature sensing applications. One possible mechanism explaining the decrease in fluorescence intensity with rising temperature is the activation of non-radiative pathways [6]. At lower temperatures, these pathways tend to remain inactive, permitting excited electrons to emit photons through radiative processes. However, as the temperature increases, a greater number of non-radiative channels become active. This leads to excited electrons returning to their ground state through non-radiative processes triggered by the vibration and rotation of emitters, ultimately resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Notably, the thermal response of the CDs was found to be reversible during temperature cycling experiments. To confirm the reversibility and restorability of the CDs’ fluorescence, we conducted an experiment measuring the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 20 °C and 80 °C after subjecting them to repeated heating-cooling cycles. As shown in the inset of Fig. 6 b, the fluorescence intensity decreased at higher temperatures (80 °C) and was subsequently restored when the temperature returned to 20 °C. Importantly, the fluorescence switching operation could be repeated for eight consecutive heating–cooling cycles without any decay, demonstrating the remarkable reversibility and restorability of the as-prepared CDs.

Fluorescent CDs have proven to be effective in optical bioimaging applications, capitalizing on their visible excitation and emission wavelengths, bright fluorescence at the individual dot level, excellent photostability, and low toxicity, as confirmed through both in vitro and in vivo assessments [4]. Here, we further explore the potential applications of the prepared CDs, such as biological imaging.

First, an MTT assay was performed to assess the cytotoxicity of the CDs [8,9,13]. The cell viability of Bcap-37 cells was tested after 24 h of incubation with different concentrations of the CDs ( Fig. 7 a). The results revealed that the cell viabilities were approximately 95.3%, 92.6%, and 92.1% for CDs concentrations of 0.05 mg/mL, 0.2 mg/mL, and 0.8 mg/mL, respectively. These findings indicate that the as-prepared CDs exhibit low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, suggesting their safety for potential in vivo applications.

To explore the potential applications of the as-prepared CDs, their use as in vitro cell imaging probes for human breast cancer Bcap-37 cells was investigated. Confocal laser scanning microscopy was employed to observe the intracellular fluorescence after incubating the cells with the CDs. Fig. 7 b presents a confocal brightfield image that displays the outline of the human breast cancer Bcap-37 cells. Upon excitation at a wavelength of 405 nm, bright blue fluorescence emitted by the CDs was observed in the cell cytoplasm region, as shown in Fig. 7 c. These results demonstrate that the as-prepared CDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility and can be effectively utilized for in vivo biological labeling and cell imaging applications. Based on previous reports, the cellular uptake of CDs involves an endocytosis mechanism, which is an active transport occurring at the cell surface and involving the internalization of extracellular materials [65–68]. The specific endocytic mechanism of CDs is likely through clathrin-mediated, caveolae-mediated, and macropinocytosis pathways. Nevertheless, additional experiments will be necessary to definitively prove such a mechanism.

A novel, straightforward, and eco-friendly method was developed to synthesize highly fluorescent CDs using L-ascorbic acid as the carbon source. The synthesis mechanism of the CDs was investigated, revealing the role of H2O2 as a green accelerator in the synthesis process. Temperature was found to play a crucial role in controlling the entire synthesis process of the CDs. The as-prepared CDs exhibited controllable fluorescence, uniform particle size distribution, excellent solubility, and remarkable stability. The PL mechanism of the CDs was explored using a time-resolved fluorescence technique, unveiling that the PL originated from two sources: surface defect state emissions, which are associated with surface functional groups, and intrinsic state emissions, linked to the carbogenic core. Notably, the PL intensity of the CDs exhibited sensitivity to pH and demonstrated linearity within the physiological pH range, suggesting their potential as pH sensors. Importantly, the CDs showed temperature-dependent fluorescence, making them a promising candidate for applications as fluorescent thermometers. The CDs also demonstrated attractive biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and excellent stability, rendering them well-suited for applications in biological imaging.

Taken together, our method is not only straightforward, highly efficient, and eco-friendly, but the resulting CDs also exhibit outstanding properties such as excellent photostability, pH sensitivity, temperature sensitivity, low toxicity, and biocompatibility. Most importantly, the CDs, endowed with both sensing and biological imaging capabilities, hold significant promise for diverse applications in various scientific and technological fields, including multimodal biomedical imaging, cancer diagnosis and treatment, drug delivery and release, cell tracking and regenerative medicine, and photothermal therapy.

Xiao-Hua Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data Curation, Visualization, Project administration, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing. Xueqin An: Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21473055).
